title: Homework 3
text: This assignment practices reading, combining, and manipulating different data types, such as Excel, comma-separated values (CSV), and databases.

title: Homework 3
text:
Glass Data
Opening this file shows that the data is delimited by commas, ,. The source of the data is here.

First we create the column names and read in the data.


glass_col_names <- c("Id", "RI", "Na", "Mg", "Al", "Si", "K", "Ca", "Ba", "Fe",
                     "Type_of_glass")

glass <- read_csv("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/~online/datasets/glass.data",
                  col_names = glass_col_names
                  )

Rows: 214 Columns: 11
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
dbl (11): Id, RI, Na, Mg, Al, Si, K, Ca, Ba, Fe, Type_of_glass

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

glass

# A tibble: 214 × 11
      Id    RI    Na    Mg    Al    Si     K    Ca    Ba    Fe Type_of_glass
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>         <dbl>
 1     1  1.52  13.6  4.49  1.1   71.8  0.06  8.75     0  0                1
 2     2  1.52  13.9  3.6   1.36  72.7  0.48  7.83     0  0                1
 3     3  1.52  13.5  3.55  1.54  73.0  0.39  7.78     0  0                1
 4     4  1.52  13.2  3.69  1.29  72.6  0.57  8.22     0  0                1
 5     5  1.52  13.3  3.62  1.24  73.1  0.55  8.07     0  0                1
 6     6  1.52  12.8  3.61  1.62  73.0  0.64  8.07     0  0.26             1
 7     7  1.52  13.3  3.6   1.14  73.1  0.58  8.17     0  0                1
 8     8  1.52  13.2  3.61  1.05  73.2  0.57  8.24     0  0                1
 9     9  1.52  14.0  3.58  1.37  72.1  0.56  8.3      0  0                1
10    10  1.52  13    3.6   1.36  73.0  0.57  8.4      0  0.11             1
# ℹ 204 more rows



Next we use a chain to overwrite the Type_of_glass variable and create a factor version of this variable. Then we replace the numbered factors with descriptive string factors.


glass_factors <- c(building_windows_float_processed = '1', 
                   building_windows_non_float_processed = '2',
                   vehicle_windows_float_processed = '3',
                   vehicle_windows_non_float_processed = '4',
                   containers = '5',
                   tableware = '6',
                   headlamps = '7')

glass <- glass |> 
            mutate("Type_of_glass" = as.factor(Type_of_glass)) |>
            mutate("Type_of_glass" = fct_recode(Type_of_glass,
                                                !!!glass_factors))

Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
ℹ In argument: `Type_of_glass = fct_recode(...)`.
Caused by warning:
! Unknown levels in `f`: 4

glass

# A tibble: 214 × 11
      Id    RI    Na    Mg    Al    Si     K    Ca    Ba    Fe Type_of_glass    
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <fct>            
 1     1  1.52  13.6  4.49  1.1   71.8  0.06  8.75     0  0    building_windows…
 2     2  1.52  13.9  3.6   1.36  72.7  0.48  7.83     0  0    building_windows…
 3     3  1.52  13.5  3.55  1.54  73.0  0.39  7.78     0  0    building_windows…
 4     4  1.52  13.2  3.69  1.29  72.6  0.57  8.22     0  0    building_windows…
 5     5  1.52  13.3  3.62  1.24  73.1  0.55  8.07     0  0    building_windows…
 6     6  1.52  12.8  3.61  1.62  73.0  0.64  8.07     0  0.26 building_windows…
 7     7  1.52  13.3  3.6   1.14  73.1  0.58  8.17     0  0    building_windows…
 8     8  1.52  13.2  3.61  1.05  73.2  0.57  8.24     0  0    building_windows…
 9     9  1.52  14.0  3.58  1.37  72.1  0.56  8.3      0  0    building_windows…
10    10  1.52  13    3.6   1.36  73.0  0.57  8.4      0  0.11 building_windows…
# ℹ 204 more rows


Note that while a warning is given regarding the factor level 4, viz., vehicle_windows_non_float_processed, this is merely because this factor, as indicated in the file description, has no observations in this database.

Finally, we purge the database of all observations except those where the iron, Fe, is less than \(0.2\) and the Type_of_glass is either tableware or headlamps.


glass <- glass |>
            filter((Fe < 0.3) & 
                      (Type_of_glass %in% c("tableware", "headlamps")))

glass

# A tibble: 38 × 11
      Id    RI    Na    Mg    Al    Si     K    Ca    Ba    Fe Type_of_glass
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <fct>        
 1   177  1.52  14    2.39  1.56  72.4  0     9.57  0        0 tableware    
 2   178  1.52  13.8  2.41  1.19  72.8  0     9.77  0        0 tableware    
 3   179  1.52  14.5  2.24  1.62  72.4  0     9.26  0        0 tableware    
 4   180  1.52  14.1  2.19  1.66  72.7  0     9.32  0        0 tableware    
 5   181  1.51  14.4  1.74  1.54  74.6  0     7.59  0        0 tableware    
 6   182  1.52  15.0  0.78  1.74  72.5  0     9.95  0        0 tableware    
 7   183  1.52  14.2  0     2.09  72.7  0    10.9   0        0 tableware    
 8   184  1.52  14.6  0     0.56  73.5  0    11.2   0        0 tableware    
 9   185  1.51  17.4  0     0.34  75.4  0     6.65  0        0 tableware    
10   186  1.51  13.7  3.2   1.81  72.8  1.76  5.43  1.19     0 headlamps    
# ℹ 28 more rows

title: Homework 3
text:
Yeast Data
This data set, found here, is delimited by double-spaces,  a tab, evidently white spaces, but some variables have spaces within them, so we must use the read_table function, which separates columns across any whitespace.

First we read in the data, manually assigned column names.


yeast_columns <- c("seq_name", "mcg", "gvh", "alm", "mit", "erl", "pox", "vac",
                   "nuc", "class")

yeast <- read_table("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/online/datasets/yeast.data",
                    col_names = yeast_columns)


── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cols(
  seq_name = col_character(),
  mcg = col_double(),
  gvh = col_double(),
  alm = col_double(),
  mit = col_double(),
  erl = col_double(),
  pox = col_double(),
  vac = col_double(),
  nuc = col_double(),
  class = col_character()
)

yeast

# A tibble: 1,484 × 10
   seq_name     mcg   gvh   alm   mit   erl   pox   vac   nuc class
   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>
 1 ADT1_YEAST  0.58  0.61  0.47  0.13   0.5   0    0.48  0.22 MIT  
 2 ADT2_YEAST  0.43  0.67  0.48  0.27   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 3 ADT3_YEAST  0.64  0.62  0.49  0.15   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 4 AAR2_YEAST  0.58  0.44  0.57  0.13   0.5   0    0.54  0.22 NUC  
 5 AATM_YEAST  0.42  0.44  0.48  0.54   0.5   0    0.48  0.22 MIT  
 6 AATC_YEAST  0.51  0.4   0.56  0.17   0.5   0.5  0.49  0.22 CYT  
 7 ABC1_YEAST  0.5   0.54  0.48  0.65   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 8 BAF1_YEAST  0.48  0.45  0.59  0.2    0.5   0    0.58  0.34 NUC  
 9 ABF2_YEAST  0.55  0.5   0.66  0.36   0.5   0    0.49  0.22 MIT  
10 ABP1_YEAST  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58  0.3  CYT  
# ℹ 1,474 more rows



Next we remove the seq_name and nuc columns.


yeast <- yeast |>
            select(-seq_name, -nuc)

yeast

# A tibble: 1,484 × 8
     mcg   gvh   alm   mit   erl   pox   vac class
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>
 1  0.58  0.61  0.47  0.13   0.5   0    0.48 MIT  
 2  0.43  0.67  0.48  0.27   0.5   0    0.53 MIT  
 3  0.64  0.62  0.49  0.15   0.5   0    0.53 MIT  
 4  0.58  0.44  0.57  0.13   0.5   0    0.54 NUC  
 5  0.42  0.44  0.48  0.54   0.5   0    0.48 MIT  
 6  0.51  0.4   0.56  0.17   0.5   0.5  0.49 CYT  
 7  0.5   0.54  0.48  0.65   0.5   0    0.53 MIT  
 8  0.48  0.45  0.59  0.2    0.5   0    0.58 NUC  
 9  0.55  0.5   0.66  0.36   0.5   0    0.49 MIT  
10  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58 CYT  
# ℹ 1,474 more rows



Finally we add columns corresponding to the mean and median of the numeric variables, mcg, gvh, alm, mit, erl, and pox at each class grouping.


yeast <- yeast |>
            group_by(class) |>
            mutate(across(mcg:vac,
                          ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE), # this part was tricky, .x is a filler for the generic row
                          .names = "mean_{.col}")) |>
            mutate(across(mcg:vac,
                          ~ median(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
                          .names = "med_{.col}"))

yeast

# A tibble: 1,484 × 22
# Groups:   class [10]
     mcg   gvh   alm   mit   erl   pox   vac class mean_mcg mean_gvh mean_alm
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
 1  0.58  0.61  0.47  0.13   0.5   0    0.48 MIT      0.521    0.533    0.517
 2  0.43  0.67  0.48  0.27   0.5   0    0.53 MIT      0.521    0.533    0.517
 3  0.64  0.62  0.49  0.15   0.5   0    0.53 MIT      0.521    0.533    0.517
 4  0.58  0.44  0.57  0.13   0.5   0    0.54 NUC      0.452    0.456    0.529
 5  0.42  0.44  0.48  0.54   0.5   0    0.48 MIT      0.521    0.533    0.517
 6  0.51  0.4   0.56  0.17   0.5   0.5  0.49 CYT      0.481    0.470    0.535
 7  0.5   0.54  0.48  0.65   0.5   0    0.53 MIT      0.521    0.533    0.517
 8  0.48  0.45  0.59  0.2    0.5   0    0.58 NUC      0.452    0.456    0.529
 9  0.55  0.5   0.66  0.36   0.5   0    0.49 MIT      0.521    0.533    0.517
10  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58 CYT      0.481    0.470    0.535
# ℹ 1,474 more rows
# ℹ 11 more variables: mean_mit <dbl>, mean_erl <dbl>, mean_pox <dbl>,
#   mean_vac <dbl>, med_mcg <dbl>, med_gvh <dbl>, med_alm <dbl>, med_mit <dbl>,
#   med_erl <dbl>, med_pox <dbl>, med_vac <dbl>


NB: The above was more complicated than it needed to be, accounting for NA values which did not exist in the original data set. They were introduced by incorrectly reading in the file with read_delim and using a double-space, , as the delimiter. Some of the observations had a space in the seq_name, causing the mcg column to begin with a space and thus interpreted as an NA.

title: Homework 3
text:
Read In and Tidy Data

First we download the file from here into our current project directory and import the data from the first sheet.


library(readxl)

Warning: package 'readxl' was built under R version 4.5.3

white_wine <- read_xlsx("white-wine.xlsx",
                        sheet = 1)

white_wine

# A tibble: 4,898 × 12
   `fixed acidity` `volatile acidity` `citric acid` `residual sugar` chlorides
             <dbl>              <dbl>         <dbl>            <dbl>     <dbl>
 1             7                 0.27          0.36             20.7     0.045
 2             6.3               0.3           0.34              1.6     0.049
 3             8.1               0.28          0.4               6.9     0.05 
 4             7.2               0.23          0.32              8.5     0.058
 5             7.2               0.23          0.32              8.5     0.058
 6             8.1               0.28          0.4               6.9     0.05 
 7             6.2               0.32          0.16              7       0.045
 8             7                 0.27          0.36             20.7     0.045
 9             6.3               0.3           0.34              1.6     0.049
10             8.1               0.22          0.43              1.5     0.044
# ℹ 4,888 more rows
# ℹ 7 more variables: `free sulfur dioxide` <dbl>,
#   `total sulfur dioxide` <dbl>, density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>,
#   alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>



Many of the variables have non-standard names and must be referenced using a back-tick character, `. To remedy this, we will read in the second sheet, which contains a column of standard names of these variables and then we will overwrite the previous variable names.


wine_col_names <- read_xlsx("white-wine.xlsx",
                            sheet = "Sheet2",
                            col_names = FALSE)

New names:
• `` -> `...1`

colnames(white_wine) <- simplify(wine_col_names) # to convert to vector

white_wine

# A tibble: 4,898 × 12
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1           7               0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 2           6.3             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
 3           8.1             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 4           7.2             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 5           7.2             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 6           8.1             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 7           6.2             0.32        0.16            7       0.045
 8           7               0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 9           6.3             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
10           8.1             0.22        0.43            1.5     0.044
# ℹ 4,888 more rows
# ℹ 7 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>


(Note: The current file on the NCSU server doesn’t have a Sheet2, so I added it manually to my local file.)

Then we add a column, distinguishing this set of data as exclusively white wine before we begin to merge it with the red wine data set.


white_wine <- white_wine |>
                mutate(type = "white")

white_wine

# A tibble: 4,898 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1           7               0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 2           6.3             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
 3           8.1             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 4           7.2             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 5           7.2             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 6           8.1             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 7           6.2             0.32        0.16            7       0.045
 8           7               0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 9           6.3             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
10           8.1             0.22        0.43            1.5     0.044
# ℹ 4,888 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>



Now we download the red wine data here and save it locally. Then we read in the data, delimited by semicolon, ;, make sure the columns have the same standard names as before, and append a type column to distinguish it from the white wine upon merging.


wine_col_types <- rep("d", times = 12)

red_wine <- read_delim("red-wine.csv",
                       delim = ";",
                       col_types = wine_col_types,
                       col_names = simplify(wine_col_names),
                       skip = 1)

red_wine <- red_wine |>
              mutate(type = "red")

red_wine

# A tibble: 1,599 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1           7.4             0.7         0               1.9     0.076
 2           7.8             0.88        0               2.6     0.098
 3           7.8             0.76        0.04            2.3     0.092
 4          11.2             0.28        0.56            1.9     0.075
 5           7.4             0.7         0               1.9     0.076
 6           7.4             0.66        0               1.8     0.075
 7           7.9             0.6         0.06            1.6     0.069
 8           7.3             0.65        0               1.2     0.065
 9           7.8             0.58        0.02            2       0.073
10           7.5             0.5         0.36            6.1     0.071
# ℹ 1,589 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>


Note that read_csv2 is not appropriate here because although it does delimit by semicolon, ;, it also assumes commas, ,, take the place of decimals, which is not appropriate to this (American) data set.

Finally, we combine the two sets of wine data.


wine_data <- bind_rows(white_wine, red_wine)

wine_data

# A tibble: 6,497 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1           7               0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 2           6.3             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
 3           8.1             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 4           7.2             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 5           7.2             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 6           8.1             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 7           6.2             0.32        0.16            7       0.045
 8           7               0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 9           6.3             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
10           8.1             0.22        0.43            1.5     0.044
# ℹ 6,487 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>

title: Homework 3
text:
Manipulate

We restrict our sample to “high” quality (quality > 6.5) and “reasonable” alcohol level (alcohol < 13.2).


wine_data_modified <- wine_data |>
                        filter((quality > 6.5) & (alcohol < 13.2))

wine_data_modified

# A tibble: 1,211 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1           6.6             0.16        0.4             1.5     0.044
 2           6.6             0.17        0.38            1.5     0.032
 3           6.2             0.66        0.48            1.2     0.029
 4           6.2             0.66        0.48            1.2     0.029
 5           6.4             0.31        0.38            2.9     0.038
 6           6.8             0.26        0.42            1.7     0.049
 7           7.2             0.32        0.36            2       0.033
 8           7.4             0.18        0.31            1.4     0.058
 9           6.6             0.25        0.29            1.1     0.068
10           6.2             0.16        0.33            1.1     0.057
# ℹ 1,201 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>


(Note: Since the maximum alcohol level is \(14.9\), I assumed you meant \(13.2\) instead of \(132\).)

Now we sort from highest to lowest quality.


wine_data_modified <- wine_data_modified |>
                        arrange(desc(quality)) # arrange descending

wine_data_modified

# A tibble: 1,211 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1           9.1             0.27        0.45           10.6     0.035
 2           6.6             0.36        0.29            1.6     0.021
 3           7.4             0.24        0.36            2       0.031
 4           6.9             0.36        0.34            4.2     0.018
 5           7.1             0.26        0.49            2.2     0.032
 6           6.2             0.66        0.48            1.2     0.029
 7           6.2             0.66        0.48            1.2     0.029
 8           6.8             0.26        0.42            1.7     0.049
 9           6.7             0.23        0.31            2.1     0.046
10           6.7             0.23        0.31            2.1     0.046
# ℹ 1,201 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>



Now we look at just the variables containing acid, alcohol, type, and quality.


wine_data_modified <- wine_data_modified |>
                        select(contains("acid"), 
                               alcohol, type, quality)

wine_data_modified

# A tibble: 1,211 × 6
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid alcohol type  quality
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>   <dbl>
 1           9.1             0.27        0.45    10.4 white       9
 2           6.6             0.36        0.29    12.4 white       9
 3           7.4             0.24        0.36    12.5 white       9
 4           6.9             0.36        0.34    12.7 white       9
 5           7.1             0.26        0.49    12.9 white       9
 6           6.2             0.66        0.48    12.8 white       8
 7           6.2             0.66        0.48    12.8 white       8
 8           6.8             0.26        0.42    10.5 white       8
 9           6.7             0.23        0.31    10.7 white       8
10           6.7             0.23        0.31    10.7 white       8
# ℹ 1,201 more rows



Lastly, for each level of quality, we find the mean and standard deviation of the alcohol variable.


wine_data_final <- wine_data_modified |>
                     group_by(quality) |>
                     mutate(across(alcohol, mean, .names = "mean_alc")) |>
                     mutate(across(alcohol, sd, .names = "sd_alc"))

wine_data_final

# A tibble: 1,211 × 8
# Groups:   quality [3]
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid alcohol type  quality mean_alc
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>   <dbl>    <dbl>
